text: Robotic right hepatectomy with portal vein thrombectomy for colorectal liver metastasis is presented. A sixty-one-year-old female underwent open resection of transverse colon cancer in December twenty-fifteen. Pathology was T-three-N-zero. In March twenty-nineteen, she underwent a non-anatomical resection of liver metastasis in segment six, also by laparotomy. She then underwent adjuvant chemotherapy. During follow-up, in September twenty-twenty, liver occurrence with a tumor thrombus in the right portal vein was diagnosed and she was referred to us. Multidisciplinary team decided for upfront liver resection due to the risk of left portal vein progression. MRI shows a large tumor in the right liver and tumoral thrombus in the portal vein.

Liver volumetry showed future liver remnant of fifty two point five percent. Right hepatectomy with portal vein thrombectomy was indicated. Robotic approach was proposed, and consent was obtained. Five trochars were used as displayed.
The video shows the previous laparotomy incision for colon resection and the laparotomy incision for liver resection. Additionally, the postoperative view of the trochar incisions is visible.

The operation begins with the division of adhesions from previous laparotomies. The hepatic hilum is carefully dissected, and the right hepatic artery is encircled, ligated, and divided. The left portal vein is encircled with a vessel loop, and the common bile duct is retracted to expose the portal vein. The portal vein trunk is encircled, and the right portal vein is dissected, revealing an enlarged vein with a protruding tumoral thrombus. The future line of right portal vein transection is shown, and the left portal vein is temporarily clamped. The right portal vein is transected with robotic scissors, leaving a minimum stump for safe suturing. Continuous backflow bleeding is observed from the right portal vein stump, which is closed with a running 5-0 Prolene suture. Clamps are removed from the left portal vein and portal vein trunk, and the anastomosis shows no leak. A gauze is introduced to stop backflow bleeding. Liver mobilization resumes with blunt dissection, the coronary ligament is dissected, and the right hepatic vein is identified. Right liver ischemic discoloration is confirmed with fluorescence imaging after indocyanine green injection.

The surgeon marks the future line of transection along the ischemic area of the liver. Bipolar forceps are used under saline irrigation to perform the liver transection, controlling hepatic vein branches. An ultrasound is utilized to check surgical margins. Larger hepatic vein branches are secured with hemolock clips. The right biliary duct is identified and divided between hemolock clips as the division of liver parenchyma progresses cranially towards the right hepatic vein.

The surgeon detaches the liver from the retrohepatic vena cava, fully exposing the inferior vena cava. The right liver remains attached solely by the right hepatic vein, which is then divided using a stapler, completing the right hepatectomy.
The surgeon inspects the raw surface area of the liver for any signs of bleeding or bile leakage. The portal vein is also examined for patency and any bleeding.

The liver remnant is sutured to the abdominal wall to prevent hepatic vein kinking and liver congestion. The surgical specimen is removed through a suprapubic incision, and the abdominal cavity is drained using a closed suction drain. The surgical specimen is displayed, showing its proximity to the right hepatic vein and the right portal vein with a protruding tumor thrombus.
